terms.title: ʿAhd (عهد)
terms.summary:
ʿAhd names a solemn binding commitment. In Qur’anic usage it may emphasize:

- An obligation **imposed** or **taken** (including divine charge upon servants or prophets)
- A pledge humans make and must fulfill
- Allah’s own faithful commitment (where context indicates)

Classical lexical notes often contrast ʿahd (commitment/obligation) with mīthāq (confirmed, mutual compact)—but the Qur’an sometimes uses them in overlapping contexts. Always read locally.
terms.html:
**Root:** ʿayn–hā–dāl

**Gloss:** covenant, pledge, commitment, charge, compact

Sense in this corpus

ʿAhd names a solemn binding commitment. In Qur’anic usage it may emphasize:

  • An obligation **imposed** or **taken** (including divine charge upon servants or prophets)
  • A pledge humans make and must fulfill
  • Allah’s own faithful commitment (where context indicates)

Classical lexical notes often contrast ʿahd (commitment/obligation) with mīthāq (confirmed, mutual compact)—but the Qur’an sometimes uses them in overlapping contexts. Always read locally.

Representative loci

Doctrinal use

  • **Ethical:** fulfilling ʿahd is piety; breaking it is major censure (Q 2:27; 13:25; 16:91; 17:34)
  • **Heilsgeschichte / salvation history:** Banī Isrāʾīl charged to fulfill reciprocal ʿahd (Q 2:40)
  • **Prophetology:** charges upon Ādam and the prophets (Q 20:115; with Q 33:7)

See also

[mithaq.md](mithaq.md) · [wad.md](wad.md) · [sources/quran-index.md](../sources/quran-index.md)

terms.title: Amānah (أمانة)
terms.summary: Amānah is the **trust** placed with a person—often linked to moral reliability and to the cosmic trust humanity bears. It intersects covenant theology as the *content* of responsibility under the divine–human bond.
terms.html:
**Root:** hamza–mīm–nūn

**Gloss:** trust, deposit, fiduciary charge

Sense in this corpus

Amānah is the **trust** placed with a person—often linked to moral reliability and to the cosmic trust humanity bears. It intersects covenant theology as the *content* of responsibility under the divine–human bond.

Representative loci

Doctrinal use

  • Ethical: betrayal of trusts is betrayal in the covenantal field
  • Cosmic: Q 33:72 frames amānah as a weighty charge (tafsīr differs on exact referent—record as **disputed** detail)
  • Paired with covenant-keeping in descriptions of the successful believer (Q 23:8)

See also

[ahd.md](ahd.md) · Level 02 hadith dossier on amānah

terms.title: Bayʿah (بيعة)
terms.summary: Bayʿah is the **pledge** given to the Prophet ﷺ (and later to legitimate leadership in Islamic political thought). It is a primary *lived* form of covenant between Messenger and community (Axis 4).
terms.html:
**Root:** bā–yā–ʿayn

**Gloss:** pledge of allegiance; contractual pledge of loyalty

Sense in this corpus

Bayʿah is the **pledge** given to the Prophet ﷺ (and later to legitimate leadership in Islamic political thought). It is a primary *lived* form of covenant between Messenger and community (Axis 4).

Qur’anic anchors

Sunnah field

Bayʿah upon Islam, upon hearing and obeying, upon the conditions of women, and related pledges appear extensively in Bukhārī and Muslim. See [`sources/hadith-index.md`](../sources/hadith-index.md) and Level 02.

Doctrinal use

  • Links personal faith commitment to communal prophetic authority
  • Q 48:10 theologically elevates bayʿah to the Messenger as bayʿah to Allah
  • Distinct from the primordial Alast mīthāq, yet both structure obligation

See also

[ahd.md](ahd.md) · Level 02 bayʿah dossier

terms.title: Dhimma (ذمة)
terms.summary:
Dhimma names a **protective covenantal status / liability of care**—historically applied in Islamic governance to protected non-Muslim subjects under treaty terms, and more broadly to any inviolable pact-based responsibility.

In theology of coexistence, dhimma is Axis 4/5 adjacent: human treaties under divine ethic of fidelity (Q 16:91; Q 9:4 regarding treaty-keepers, etc.).
terms.html:
**Root:** dhāl–mīm–mīm

**Gloss:** covenant of protection; liability; inviolable care owed under pact

Sense in this corpus

Dhimma names a **protective covenantal status / liability of care**—historically applied in Islamic governance to protected non-Muslim subjects under treaty terms, and more broadly to any inviolable pact-based responsibility.

In theology of coexistence, dhimma is Axis 4/5 adjacent: human treaties under divine ethic of fidelity (Q 16:91; Q 9:4 regarding treaty-keepers, etc.).

Notes on sources

  • Qur’anic treaty ethics: keep covenants with those who keep them; rules for breach and hostility appear in Sūrat al-Tawbah and elsewhere—read with tafsīr, not slogans
  • [history][fiqh] Classical dhimma regulations are juristic-historical applications—not identical to revelation itself
  • This corpus treats dhimma as **covenantal ethics of pledged protection**, citing primary ethics first

Doctrinal use

  • Fidelity to treaties is covenantal piety
  • Breach of pledged protection is grave in prophetic teaching (see Level 02 hadith index)
  • Avoid modern polemical framing; stay with sources and classical categories

See also

[ahd.md](ahd.md) · Level 02 Madinah practice · Level 04 political/social covenants chapter

terms.title: Mīthāq (ميثاق)
terms.summary:
Mīthāq typically signals a **confirmed** covenant—something taken, ratified, or mutually binding. Classical notes often stress reciprocity more than for ʿahd, though contexts vary.

The **locus classicus** for the primordial human covenant is **Q 7:172** (Alast / *a-lastu bi-rabbikum*).
terms.html:
**Root:** wāw–thā–qāf

**Gloss:** covenant, compact, confirmed pact (often implying ratification / mutual bond)

Sense in this corpus

Mīthāq typically signals a **confirmed** covenant—something taken, ratified, or mutually binding. Classical notes often stress reciprocity more than for ʿahd, though contexts vary.

The **locus classicus** for the primordial human covenant is **Q 7:172** (Alast / *a-lastu bi-rabbikum*).

Representative loci

Doctrinal use

  • **Primordial theology:** Alast grounds human accountability (Q 7:172–173)
  • **Prophetology:** mīthāq al-nabiyyīn (Q 3:81; Q 33:7)
  • **Scripture communities:** repeated remonstrance for breaking mīthāq
  • **Family law:** marriage as mīthāq ghalīẓ (Q 4:21)

See also

[ahd.md](ahd.md) · Level 01 Alast chapter · [sources/quran-index.md](../sources/quran-index.md)

terms.title: ʿUqūd (عقود)
terms.summary: ʿUqūd are **binding contracts**. Qur’anic command to fulfill contracts situates commercial, social, and personal agreements inside the ethic of covenant-keeping—without collapsing every contract into the primordial mīthāq.
terms.html:
**Root:** ʿayn–qāf–dāl

**Gloss:** contracts, knots, binding agreements

Sense in this corpus

ʿUqūd are **binding contracts**. Qur’anic command to fulfill contracts situates commercial, social, and personal agreements inside the ethic of covenant-keeping—without collapsing every contract into the primordial mīthāq.

Primary locus

Related

Marriage as *mīthāq ghalīẓ* (Q 4:21) shows family bonds using covenantal vocabulary. Oaths (*aymān*) reinforce contracts (see [related.md](related.md)).

Doctrinal / ethical use

  • Fulfilling ʿuqūd is a mark of faith practice
  • [fiqh] develops detailed contract law; this corpus tracks the *theological ethic*, not a furūʿ manual
  • Distinguish: **legal ʿaqd** vs **theological mīthāq** (Level 03)

See also

[mithaq.md](mithaq.md) · [ahd.md](ahd.md)

terms.title: Waʿd (وعد)
terms.summary: Waʿd is a **promise**—divine or human. It belongs to the covenantal *field* because covenants often contain or rest on promises, but waʿd is not identical to ʿahd/mīthāq.
terms.html:
**Root:** wāw–ʿayn–dāl

**Gloss:** promise

Sense in this corpus

Waʿd is a **promise**—divine or human. It belongs to the covenantal *field* because covenants often contain or rest on promises, but waʿd is not identical to ʿahd/mīthāq.

Representative loci

Doctrinal use

  • Allah’s waʿd is truthful and unbroken
  • Human claims of special waʿd without warrant are censured
  • Eschatological hope is framed as divine promise within the wider covenantal bond

See also

[ahd.md](ahd.md) · [mithaq.md](mithaq.md)
